coredata.dc:description: Cardiovascular diseases have become the number one killer of human health. Early diagnosis and treatment of cardiovascular diseases can effectively improve the treatment effect and reduce the disease death rate. As an important marker of cardiovascular disease, Low-Density Lipoprotein (LDL) can be used for the early detection of cardiovascular disease. In this study, a novel carbon-based Light Addressable Potentiometric Sensor (C-LAPS) for determination LDL has been constructed based on o-phenylenediamine modified nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/reduce graphene oxide (OPD@NGQDs/rGO) photosensitive layer. The OPD@NGQDs/rGO photosensitive layer has excellent photoelectric conversion efficiency, large specific surface area and good biocompatibility to enhance the response signal. For the determination LDL by the C-LAPS system, LDL binding to LDL aptamer causes a change in the surface potential of the photosensitive layer, producing a response signal that can be detected by the I-V curve. Under optimal conditions, the C-LAPS had a good linear relationship with LDL concentration in the range of 0.01 μg/mL- 0.15 μg/mL. The sensitivity was 1819 mV/µg·mL−1 and the limit of detection (LOD) was 6.43 ng/mL (S/N = 3). In addition, the C-LAPS showed good specificity, reproducibility, stability and satisfactory relative error for detecting LDL in human serum samples. Therefore, the C-LAPS has good performance and demonstrates potential clinical value.

head.ce:title: Carbon-based light addressable potential sensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for detection of low-density lipoprotein

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel carbon-based Light Addressable Potentiometric Sensor (C-LAPS) for determination LDL has been constructed based on o-phenylenediamine modified nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/reduce graphene oxide as photosensitive layer and LDL aptamer as recognition molecule.

Cardiovascular diseases have become the number one killer of human health. Early diagnosis and treatment of cardiovascular diseases can effectively improve the treatment effect and reduce the disease death rate. As an important marker of cardiovascular disease, Low-Density Lipoprotein (LDL) can be used for the early detection of cardiovascular disease. In this study, a novel carbon-based Light Addressable Potentiometric Sensor (C-LAPS) for determination LDL has been constructed based on o-phenylenediamine modified nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/reduce graphene oxide (OPD@NGQDs/rGO) photosensitive layer. The OPD@NGQDs/rGO photosensitive layer has excellent photoelectric conversion efficiency, large specific surface area and good biocompatibility to enhance the response signal. For the determination LDL by the C-LAPS system, LDL binding to LDL aptamer causes a change in the surface potential of the photosensitive layer, producing a response signal that can be detected by the I-V curve. Under optimal conditions, the C-LAPS had a good linear relationship with LDL concentration in the range of 0.01 μg/mL- 0.15 μg/mL. The sensitivity was 1819 mV/µg·mL−1 and the limit of detection (LOD) was 6.43 ng/mL (S/N = 3). In addition, the C-LAPS showed good specificity, reproducibility, stability and satisfactory relative error for detecting LDL in human serum samples. Therefore, the C-LAPS has good performance and demonstrates potential clinical value.

ce:keyword.ce:text: O-phenylenediamine modified nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Each year, 20 million people die from cardiovascular diseases (CVD), which are the main factor of human mortality [1]. Cheap and accurate early detection of CVD is an effective way to reduce the risk of cardiovascular. C-reactive protein (CRP), triglycerides (TC), Low-density lipoprotein (LDL) and high-density lipoprotein (HDL) are generally used to assess the risk of CVD [2,3]. Among them, LDL is a kind of lipid transport protein which is mainly composed of cholesterol esters, phospholipids and Apolipoprotein B (AB). LDL is responsible for transporting cholesterol and lipids from the liver to other organs via blood vessels, and has the effect of affecting the blood lipid content of the human body [4,5].The LDL concentration in the a normal person blood is not exceed 100 mg/dL. And higher LDL will lead to fatty deposits on blood vessels forming atherosclerosis, with a 10 % increase in CVD risk for each standard deviation increase in LDL levels which is considered a direct cause of CVD[6,7]. Some studies have also shown that the risk of CVD reduction is proportional to the magnitude of LDL reduction, and interventions for LDL concentrations can be effective in preventing the occurrence of CVD and reducing symptoms[8–10]. Therefore, LDL is considered to be a key marker in the diagnosis of CVD, especially for the Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease (ASCVD), and it is of great value to develop for LDL detection.

The common methods for LDL detection include enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA), Friedewald method, electrophoresis, immunoseparation, centrifugation, and so on [11–13]. Although these traditional detection have higher accuracy and better sensitivity, these methods require professionals and specialized instruments, which have the disadvantages of being time-consuming and costly, and are not suitable as a means of early screening and real-time detection [14]. To solve these problems, researchers have turned their attention to electrochemical sensor [15]. For example, Kuar et al. immobilized Anti-Apolipoprotein B (AAB) on a NiO film to propose an AAB/NiO/ITO label-free electrochemical immunosensor, which has an impedance response sensitivity of up to 12 kΩ.μM−1 for LDL [16]. Ali et al. prepared an AAB/CysCdSQDs-NiO electrochemical immunosensor for the detection of LDL in serum with a minimum detection limit of 0.05 mg/dL using AAB coupled with L-cysteine encapsulated cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CysCdSQDs) combined with NiO nanorods (nNiO) [17]. Our group constructed an LDL electrochemical immunosensor based on the principle of dual enzyme-catalyzed silver deposition by specifically capturing LDL onto the surface of a glassy carbon electrode using AAB, the cholesterol esterase and cholesterol oxidase dual enzymes hydrolyze LDL cholesterol (LDL-C) and produce weakly reducing H2O2, which in turn reduces Ag+ to Ag0 and deposits it on the electrode surface, and the quantitative detection of LDL-C is achieved with a minimum detection limit of 0.36 ng/mL [18]. However, most of the current sensors for LDL use AAB as recognition elements for LDL detection. The antibody preparation cycle is long, the purity requirement is high, and it is expensive and time-consuming, which makes it difficult to meet the clinical requirements for LDL detection. Thus, there is a need to find new recognition element with low cost, operation simple, high specificity and affinity with LDL.

Nucleic acid aptamers are ssDNA or RNA molecules that recognize specifically target molecules with unique tertiary structure, screened from random oligonucleotide libraries by systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) [19]. Because aptamers are generated by artificial selection and not necessary for immunogenicity to activate host immune system, aptamers can specifically bind to ions, small molecules, peptides, RNA, proteins, cells, organs [20]. The aptamers bind tightly to the target substance through electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bonding and base complementary pairing, and has high specificity and affinity. In addition, aptamers are generally chemically synthesized, making them easy to be modified and conjugated with other carriers. The use of aptamers as markers is an effective means to improve the performance because of their advantages of storage resistance, stable binding and specificity [21]. Many researchers have screened LDL aptamer by SELEX technology and developed many methods for LDL detection using LDL aptamer as recognition element. For instance, Wang et al. screened a DNA oligonucleotide with high specificity and affinity for LDL (KD = 19.6 nM) using the SELEX [22]. Klapak et al. screened a class of LDL aptamer with high affinity and used the LDL aptamer as detection probe for LDL detection [23]. Rudewicz-Kowalczyk et al. have compared electrochemical immunosensor with electrochemical aptamer sensor for the detection of LDL, and the electrochemical aptamer sensor using ssDNA as the recognition unit demonstrated better specificity and lower detection limits of 0.25 ng/mL under optimal conditions [24].

Light addressable potential sensor (LAPS) is a kind of semiconductor electrochemical sensor with the advantages of small size, easy operation and high sensitivity, which has been used to develop various portable sensors [25]. Differing from the voltammetric sensor, LAPS featured with an insulator layer has a high input impedance which can help to avoid the disturbance on the chemical equilibrium of the electrolyte. LAPS measures potential changes of electrolyte/insulator interface by using a modulated light to illuminate the depleted semiconductor to generate local alternating photocurrents[26,27]. When LAPS is illuminated by an excitation light source, a photocurrent controlled by the surface potential is generated, and a current-voltage (I-V) curve can be obtained by the correspondence between the potential and the photocurrent. When a measured substance is present on the surface of LAPS, the potential will change and cause a shift in the I-V curve, different concentration of measured substance will cause different potential shift [28,29]. Thus, LAPS can be used to detect different substance such as ion, biomarker, cell, and so on.

The conventional LAPS uses silicon-based materials as the photosensitive layer, which has the disadvantages of high cost and poor mechanical properties[30]. In recent years, carbon-based materials such as carbon nanotubes, carbon quantum dots, graphene quantum dots, fullerene (C60) and so on, have been widely used to develop carbon-based LAPS (C-LAPS), which have the advantages of easy processing, low cost and flexible devices [31,32]. For instance, Li et al. constructed Anti-5mc sensors based on graphene/carbon dot photosensitive layers, which demonstrated good sensitivity and detection limits, and shows good linearity in the range of 0–0.5 µg/mL[31]. A C60-based LAPS sensor for pH detection and electrochemical imaging was developed by Li et al, the proposed C60-based LAPS exhibited an excellent pH sensitivity of 150 mV/pH as well as a high spatial resolution of 3.8 μm [32]. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are an ideal material to replace silicon-based photosensitive layers. Graphene is a zero-bandgap semiconductor and a two-dimensional material with a hexagonal structure of carbon atoms in sp2 hybridized orbitals, while GQDs are zero-dimensional materials with a graphene sheet size below 100 nm [33]. GQDs can be compounded with graphene to form a zero-dimensional/2-dimensional material by Van der Waals Heterojunction at the interface. According to the energy band theory, in the presence of bias voltage, photoexcited electrons from GQDs are transferred to adjacent graphene by Van der Waals Heterojunction, thus forming photogenerated carriers, which in turn generate detectable photocurrents in the outer loop [34]. The sensitivity of LAPS sensors is closely related to the light absorption ability, photocarrier generation efficiency, charge transfer resistance and average carrier migration distance of the photosensitive layer, while the performance of the carbon-based photosensitive layers depends on the fluorescence performance of GQDs [35,36]. Therefore, how to enhance the performance of GQDs is the key to prepare carbon-based LAPS with good sensitivity. Heteroatomic doping is one of the means to enhance the performance of GQDs because heteroatomic doping helps to reduce the charge transfer resistance, promote hole separation, and enhance the light absorption ability [37]. The common ones are nitrogen atom doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), sulfur atom doped graphene quantum dots (SGQDs), etc. However, heteroatomic doping may cause blue-shifting of GQDs excitation peaks to the UV band, which limits the choice of light sources and is not conducive to the miniaturization of sensors [38]. Yan et al. conjugated GQDs with different polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons to achieve continuous adjustment of excitation peaks, and used them for photocatalytic decomposition of water and CO2 reduction [39]. In addition, o-phenylenediamine (OPD), a typical aniline derivative, has two adjacent amino groups at the benzene ring. Therefore, OPD can provide more active sites to be further modified [40]. Herein, we have prepared OPD modified nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (OPD@NGQDs) to narrow the band gap of NGQDs, which would help to improve the light absorption ability of NGQDs and enhance the sensitivity of LAPS sensors.

Inspired by the above, a carbon-based aptamer LAPS using o-phenylenediamine modified nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and reduce graphene oxide (OPD@NGQDs/rGO) as the photosensitive layer and Indium Tin Oxide (ITO) as the conductive substrate was designed for LDL detection. Compared with the conventional silicon electrode, using ITO as the substrate has the advantages of simple preparation and low cost. By utilizing the high conductivity and excellent fluorescence property of OPD@NGQDs with the excitation peak at 405 nm, we constructed an OPD@NGQDs/rGO photosensitive layer with high photoelectric conversion efficiency, which can effectively improve the sensitivity of the sensor while free from the limitation of the light source. In addition, the large specific surface area of OPD@NGQDs/rGO and the large number of carboxyl groups on the surface provide a large number of binding sites to effectively immobilize LDL aptamer (LDLApt). When LDLApt binds to LDL through specific recognition reaction, the spatial structure changes, resulting in the change of the thickness of the double electric layer at the sensing interface of LAPS. The change in the thickness of the bilayer changes the magnitude of the photocurrent at a fixed bias voltage and causes a shift in the I-V curve. The C-LAPS was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The analytical performance and specificity, stability and reproducibility of the C-LAPS are discussed.

Single-layer graphene oxide (GO) was obtained from XianFeng Nano Materials Tech Co.Ltd (Nanjing, China). Citric Acid (CA) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co.Ltd (Shanghai, China). Urea, Ascorbic acid (AA), Ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O) were obtained from Aladin Biochemical Technology Co.Ltd (Shanghai, China). o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and dimethyl Formamide (DMF) were provided by Xilongs Scientific Co.Ltd (Guangdong, China). Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) was provided by Macklin Co.,Ltd (Shanghai, China). Low density Lipoprotein (LDL) were provided by Solarbio life sciences Co.Ltd (Beijing, China). A LDL aptamer (LDLApt) with the following sequence (5′–NH2-ACCTCGATTTTATATTATTTCGCTTACCAACAACTGCAGA-3′[23])was provided from Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Three-electrode system was used on C-LAPS system to detect LDL with functionalized tested C-LAPS chip as the working electrode, the Pt electrode as counter electrode and the Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. Laser Diode (LD) (405 nm, 10 mW) was chosen as the light source to illuminate the LAPS chip to produce photocurrent. Virtual instrument based on LabVIEW (National Instruments, Beijing, China) was developed to provide the driven signals and to acquire, process, analyze the samples data. The current-voltage (I-V) curve data was normalized of the photocurrent measured in the device accumulation region (bias voltage). Normalization is a data calculation method that translates a measured scale into an unmeasured scale, mapping all the data to the interval of [0,1]. The normalized data not only preserves the original information of the I-V curve, but also allows comparison between the different curves. The shifts of I-V curve (referred as potential shifts, △V) originated from local surface potential changes were recorded to reflect the changes of LDL concentration [28,29]. Each experiment was performed in triplicates and data were expressed as the mean ± relative standard deviation (RSD).

The morphology and structure of the prepared OPD@NGQDs and C-LAPS were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Quanta 200, Elementar, Germany), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TECNAI F20 EM, FEI, USA), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Bruker Tensor 27, Bruker, Germany), Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis, UH5300, HITACHI, Japan) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, ThermoFisher, USA).

OPD@NGQDs was prepared using following process: Firstly, 1 mM CA and 3 mM Uric acid was mixed with 20 mL pure water, and stirred until the precipitation disappeared. The mix solution was transferred to a reactor, heated to 180 ℃ and held for 6 h. Then, the solution was cooled to room temperature, washed by centrifugation (9000 rpm, 10 min) with a large amount of ethanol, and freeze-dried (-80 ℃, 24 h) to obtain NGQDs freezed powder. Secondly, 10 mg NGQDs and 30 mg OPD was added into 50 mL DMF, stirred to form uniform mixture, which was transferred to a reactor, and heated to 200 ℃ and remained for 12 h. Finally, the solution was treated by dialysis (500 Da, 24 h) to remove impurities, and freeze-dried (-80 ℃, 24 h) to obtain OPD@NGQDs powder.

rGO solution was prepared using following process: Firstly, 10.0 mg single layer GO powder was added to 10.0 mL distilled water to make 1.0 mg/mL GO dispersion after ultrasonically disrupt by cell disruptor (1 h). Then, 100 mg AA was added to GO dispersion, and the dispersion was placed on a constant-temperature magnetic agitator at 25 ℃ for 6–12 h to obtain rGO solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the functionalized tested C-LAPS chip
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, ITO glass electrodes were cut into 1.5 cm × 2.5 cm pieces and ultrasonically cleaned in ethanol, acetone, and deionized water for 15 min, respectively. Secondly, ITO electrodes were immersed in a mix solution of H2O2 (30 %), NH4OH (25 %), and H2O with a volume ratio of 1:1:5 for 30 min. Thirdly, ITO electrodes were washed clean with deionized water and incubated in APTES solution (5 %) for 1 h, washed with deionized water and dried. Fourthly, 20 μL of rGO (1.0 mg/mL) was dropwised onto the ITO electrodes and dried at 25 °C in incubator. Fifthly, 20 μL of OPD@NGQDs (0.5 mg/mL) solution was dropwised onto the rGO/ITO electrode and fixed for 30 min at 25 °C in incubator to obtain the C-LAPS chip. Sixthly, 10 μL of LDLApt (0.5 mM) was dropwised on the C-LAPS chip, incubated for 30 min at 25 °C to achieve specific recognition of LDL, washed with deionized water after incubation. Finally, 10.0 μL 1 % BSA solution was incubated to the LDLApt/OPD@NGQDs/rGO/C-LAPS surface to block nonspecific binding sites, therefore a working electrode of C-LAPS for blank group was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Set up the C-LAPS detection system and determination of LDL
ce:section.ce:para: For electrochemical measurements, 5.0 μL of different concentrations of LDL solution was dropped onto the suface of the functionalized tested C-LAPS chip, incubated in an oscillating incubator at 25 °C for 80 min, washed with phosphate buffer solution (PBS), dried to obtain the working electrode for the experiment group. Then, the electrolytic cell is fixed to the the working electrode by conductive adhesive and placed in the C-LAPS detection system, and 0.3 mL electrolyte solution (PBS, 0.1 mol/L, pH = 6.5) is added to the electrolytic cell. Finally, the Ag/AgCl reference electrode and the counter Pt electrode were placed in the electrolytic cell. When a certain bias voltage (from −0.6 to 2.0 V) is applied between the reference electrode and the working electrode, a depletion layer is produced at the interface between the ITO substrate and the insulating layer. The bottom of the working electrode is illuminated by the external Laser Diode (LD) (405 nm, 10 mW) to generate photogenerated electron hole, which is diffused and accumulated to the depletion layer, thus forms the weak photocurrent signal. The weak photocurrent signal is transformed into a recognizable voltage signal by the trans-impedance amplifier circuit and collected by the data acquisition card. The data recording process is controlled and displayed by LabVIEW software. The I-V curves data was acquired through the constructed LAPS detection system, and the potential shift in the I-V curves between the blank group and the experiment group was recorded for quantitative analysis of the LDL content in the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of LDL in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were collected from the 924th Hospital of the People’s Liberation Army Joint Logistics Support Force (Guilin, China) after obtaining the approval from the Ethics Committee of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Metabolic Disease Research. At first, the human serum samples were diluted with PBS at a certain ratio, 5.0 μL of the each diluted serum sample was dropwised on the surface of the functionalized tested C-LAPS chip, incubated at 25 ℃ for 80 min, washed three times with PBS and dried to obtain the tested C-LAPS chip. Then, the tested C-LAPS chip was inset the constructed C-LAPS system and I-V curve were monitored. Finally, the LDL concentration in the samples was calculated through the standard curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection principle and feasibility analysis of C-LAPS based on OPD@NGQDs and aptamer for the LDL determination
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a novel C-LAPS for the LDL determination was designed based on OPD@NGQDs and aptamer, and its analytical principle was shown in Fig. 1 A. Firstly, a layer of rGO was dropwised on the surface of ITO. Secondly, OPD@NGQDs were deposited on rGO/ITO as the photosensitive layers. Following that, the specific recognition factor LDLApt is loaded on the surface of the OPD@NGQDs/rGO/ITO by the dehydration condensation reaction of amino and carboxyl groups to construct LDL LAPS. In electrolyte solutions, the porous structure of rGO surface formed a unique double layer structure at the electrode/solution interface, and the thickness of the double layer affected the bias voltage at the electrode interface and thus changed the magnitude of the photocurrent. When the LDL was incubated on the surface of LDLApt/OPD@NGQDs/rGO/ITO electrode, LDLApt binded to LDL through specific recognition reaction to form the LDL-LDLApt combination, which leaded to a change in the spatial structure of the electrode surface, resulting in the change of the thickness of the double electric layer at the sensing interface of C-LAPS. At the same time, OPD@NGQDs generated detectable photocurrent under the action of bias voltage and excitation light source, and the change of surface potential caused the change of photocurrent magnitude, resulting in the shift of I-V curve.

In order to analyze the feasibility analysis of the LDL C-LAPS, I-V method was performed in PBS (0.1 mol/L, pH = 6.5) on the C-LAPS system and scanning range from −0.6 – 2.0 V. Fig. 1B was the I-V curves of C-LAPS in different process, the ITO electrode (curve a) had the lowest photocurrent at the same potential. Compared to ITO electrode, the I-V curve of rGO/ITO electrode (curve b) had a left shift due to the increase of electrochemical surface area. When OPD@NGQDs (curve c) were assembled to the rGO/ITO electrode, the I-V curve continued to drift to the left due to the photocurrent generated by the OPD@NGQDs/rGO in response to the light source and bias voltage. After LDLApt (curve d) was incubated to the OPD@NGQDs/rGO/ITO electrode, the I-V curve drifted slightly to the right because LDLApt produced a membrane-like structure on the electrode surface. While LDL (curve e) was incubated on the surface of LDLApt/OPD@NGQDs/rGO/ITO electrode, the combination of LDL and LDLApt produced a change in spatial structure, resulting in a thickening of the bilayer and a leftward shift in the I-V curve. Also, the change of photocurrent of C-LAPS in different process were detected in electrochemical workstation under chopped illumination, and the results were presented in Fig.S1 of Supplementary Information. From the above discussion, it is clear that the designed C-LAPS for LDL detection is feasible.

The UV–vis spectra are used to characterize the characteristic peaks of OPD@NGQDs. Fig. 2 A shows the UV–Vis spectra of GQDs (curve a), NGQDs (curve b) and OPD@NGQDs (curve c). GQDs, NGQDs, and OPD@NGQDs all show absorption peaks near 200 nm, which is the characteristic peak of GQDs. The characteristic peak of NGQDs at 360 nm is caused by the C-N bond in NGQDs [41]. Due to the introduction of OPDs, a strong absorption peak at 230 nm appears, corresponding to the characteristic peaks of benzene and heterocyclic rings. At the same time, the characteristic peak of C-N bond is blue-shifted due to substitution. The surface functional groups of NGQDs and NGQDs and OPD@NGQDs were characterized by FTIR, Fig. 2B was the result of it. The peaks of NGQDs (curve a) and OPD@NGQDs (curve b) which near 3017 cm−1 and 1637 cm−1 were the characteristic peak of O-H bond and C = O bond. Compared to NGQDs, the OPD@NGQDs shows a C − H band at 2900 cm−1 from the OPD, and a characteristic peak at 1409 cm−1 band is attributed to C = N between the OPD and NGQDs. OPD@NGQDs has a strong peak of 1245 cm−1, which means more heteroatoms are bound to carbon atoms. The characteristic peak of OPD@NGQDs near 750 cm−1 is the C-H bond of disubstituted benzene, while the peak at 1637 cm−1 decreases significantly due to the conjugation reaction of –NH2 of OPD with C = O of NGQDs, All these proves the successful binding of OPD with NGQDs.

The TEM image of OPD@NGQDs was shown in Fig. 2C. From Fig. 2C, OPD@NGQDs exhibited a particle size of about 5 nm and good dispersion, which could explain why OPD@NGQDs has good quantum fluorescence performance. The excitation fluorescence spectra of NGQDs and OPD@NGQDs were shown in Fig. 2D. The NGQDs (curve a) had the maximum excitation peak at 360 nm, while the maximum excitation peak of OPD@NGQDs (curve d) was shifted to 405 nm. The emission fluorescence spectra of NGQDs and OPD@NGQDs, the image of NGQDs and OPD@NGQDs under UV lamp illumination (365 nm), and the emission peak intensity under different excitation weavelength were shown in Figs. S2 – S4.

To further analyze the structure of OPD@NGQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed and the XPS spectra of NGQDs and OPD@NGQDs were shown in Fig. 3 . From Fig. 3A, the C1s, N1s and O1s of OPD@NGQDs were changed compared to NGQDs, due to the C = O reaction with –NH2, which indicated that the binding of NGQDs to OPD was successful. For the same reseason, the peak of C = O decreased in OPD@NGQDs compared to NGQDs in Fig. 3B. In addition, Fig. 3C showed the profiles of N1s of NGQDs contained mainly Pyridinic N and Pyrrolic N, while OPD@NGQDs contained Amino N and Pyrazine N, which indicated that the condensation of NGQDs with OPDs produced heterocycles and a part of the amino group was not reacted. In Fig. 3D, the O1s spectra was show that the C = O bond decreased significantly after reacted with OPD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM characterization of the LDL C-LAPS
ce:section.ce:para: F demonstrated the typical SEM images of LDL C-LAPS at different modification process. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the surface of ITO electrode was smooth. In Fig. 4B, we can see many particles on the surface, indicating the successful attachment of APTES. Compared with Fig. 4B, the electrode surface showed a sheet-like stacking, which is the typical morphology for rGO materials (Fig. 4C). After the dropwise addition of OPD@NGQDs (Fig. 4D), granular material appeared under high magnification, which may be formed after quantum dot agglomeration. In Fig. 4E, white particulate matter was observed during LDLApt assembly. In Fig. 4F, there was a more obvious formation of wrapped material, which may be due to the specific binding of LDLApt and LDL.

To achieve the best result, the conditions of the experiment were optimized in five aspects: the pH value of PBS, the concentration of rGO, the concentration of OPD@NGQDs, the concentration of LDLApt, the incubation temperature and the incubation time.

It has been previously demonstrated that pH of solution can exhibit great influence on the PL intensity of NGQDs, thus affecting sensing property. The surface groups of NGQDs were protonated in the high acidic system. And the hydrogen bonding in NGQDs would be aggregation and agglomeration in high alkaline buffer solutions [42,43]. Fig. 5 A showed the effect of the pH value of PBS on the C-LAPS. When the pH value of PBS was from 6.0 to 6.5, the ΔV value increased; when the pH value was from 6.5 to 7.4, the ΔV value decreased slowly; while when the pH value was from 7.4 to 8.0, the ΔV value decreased rapidly. The reason may be that the detection effect of LDL was affected by the presence of too much acid or too much alkali in PBS buffer. Therefore, pH = 6.5 of PBS can achieve the best detection effect.

Fig. 5B showed the effect of the concentration of rGO on the on the C-LAPS. When the concentration of rGO increased from 0 mg/mL to 1.0 mg/mL, the ΔV gradually increased from 135.4 mV to 518.3 mV. When the concentration of rGO continued to increase, the ΔV began to increase slowly. Therefore, 1.0 mg/mL was selected as the optimal concentration of rGO.

Fig. 5C showed the effect of the concentration of OPD@NGQDs. The ΔV increased rapidly from 149.5 mV to 494.5 mV when the concentration of OPD@NGQDs increased from 0 mg/mL to 0.5 mg/mL. This is because the increase in the concentration of OPD@NGQDs raises the intensity of the photocurrent. When the concentration of OPD@NGQDs exceeded 0.5 mg/mL, agglomeration of OPD@NGQDs occurred, causing a decrease in photocurrent conversion efficiency and a decrease in ΔV. Therefore, 0.5 mg/mL was selected as the optimal concentration of OPD@NGQDs.

Fig. 5D showed the effect of the LDLApt concentration on the C-LAPS. The ΔV value increased from 316.0 mV to 501.1 mV when the LDLApt concentration was changed from 0.1 μM to 0.5 μM. When the concentration increased from 0.5 μM to 1.0 μM, the ΔV value decreased rapidly. The reason was that the concentration of LDLApt was too low, the aptamer cannot bind LDL efficiently. If the concentration was too high, the excess LDLApt would increase the impedance and affect the movement of electron. Therefore, LDLApt concentration of 0.5 μM can achieve the best detection effect.

Fig. 5E depicted the effect of the incubation temperature on the C-LAPS. On the one hand, the activity of aptamer and protein are affected by the incubation temperature, which was too high or too low are detrimental to the specific recognition reaction for LDLApt with LDL. On the other hand, the temperature would effect in the light power due to the luminous efficiency loss [44]. The ΔV increased from 193.8 mV to 475.4 mV with the incubation temperature changed from 4 ℃ to 25 ℃. As the temperature over 25 ℃, ΔV greatly decreased. This is may be mainly because the concentration of electrons and holes would increase on the OPD@NGQDs/rGO photosensitive layer, and the electron mobility would decrease with the increase of temperature. Therefore, the best incubation temperature was selected on 25 ℃.

Fig. 5F showed the influence of the incubation time on the C-LAPS. The ΔV increased from 539.0 mV to 574.0 mV when the incubation time was from 10 min to 30 min. When the incubation time over 30 min, the ΔV tended to decrease. With the gradual increase of incubation time, the binding of LDL and LDLApt became more tight, while with the further extension of incubation time, the activity of LDL and LDLapt will be affected, resulting in a decrease in potential shift. Therefore, the best incubation time was selected on 30 min.

Under the optimized conditions, the prepared C-LAPS was used to detect different concentrations of LDL (0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2 µg/mL) by I-V method and the results was shown in Fig. 6 A. It can be observed from Fig. 6A that the ΔV increased rapidly with the increase of LDL concentration from 0.01 µg/mL to 0.15 µg/mL. The ΔV increased slowly when the LDL concentration over 0.2 µg/mL and stopped to increase at 0.5 µg/mL. This can be attributed to saturation of LDLApt binding capacity, and LDL forms membranes that block electron transfer. The corresponding linear calibration curve between the ΔV (mV) and LDL concentration (C, µg/mL) was shown in Fig. 6B. Seen from Fig. 6B, the ΔV had a linear fitting relationship with the concentration of LDL in the range of 0.01–0.15 µg/mL. The linear correlation equation was ΔV = 1819.42C-11.33 with R2 of 0.9902. The sensitivity was represented as the slope of the calibrated curve in Fig. 6B, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated according to the following equation [45].
(1)
LOD = N
S b
/ k
Where Sb is the standard deviation for the six blank groups (here Sb was 3.89 in the experiment), N is the signal/noise ratio (N = 3). and k is the slope of the standard curve (here k is 1819.42). Following the above definitions and equation, the sensitivity was 1819 mV/µg·mL−1, and the LOD was calculated to be 6.43 ng/mL at the signal/noise ratio N = 3.

The detection performance of the prepared C-LAPS for the detection of LDL was compared with those previously reported in the literature, and the results were shown in Table 1 . Compared with other aptamer sensors, the prepared LDL C-LAPS had similar sensitivity and detection limits while being simple to fabricate. However, the poor conductivity of LDL and LDLApt hindered electron transfer, and high LDL concentrations resulted in reduced voltage shifts, resulting in a smaller detection range for LDL. At the same time, the assay was simple to operate, and could be mass produced compared to other methods. It provides a promising method for the clinical detection of LDL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, reproducibility, and stability of the LDL C-LAPS
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the specificity of LDL C-LAPS, some kinds of interfering substances in actual human serum were selected including high density lipoprotein (HDL), human serum albumin (HSA), alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), cholesterol (CHOL) IgG and mix sample (MIX, four interferers and LDL mixed in the amount of 1:1:1:1:1). Under the optimized conditions, each interfering substance (1.0 µg/mL) was used instead of LDL (0.1 µg/mL) to assess the specificity of the LDL C-LAPS. In addition, LDL C-LAPS was constructed using random sequences (RAND) instead of LDLapt to detect LDL, and the offset was observed for comparison. As shown in Fig. 6C, when the sample were MIX, HDL, HSA, RAND, AFP, CHOL and IgG, the ΔV were 151.6 mV, 46.7 mV, 34.3 mV, 33.8 mV, −67.1 mV, −46.1 mV and –23.2 mV respectively, accounting for 84.2 %, 26.0 %, 19.0 %, 18.8 %, −37.2 %, −25.6 % and 12.9 % of the ΔV of LDL (180.1 mV). Therefore, the LDL C-LAPS has acceptable specificity to LDL.

To analyze the reproducibility of LDL C-LAPS, seven groups of C-LAPS with the same treatment at the same time were used to detect the same LDL concentration under same conditions, and each LAPS chip was measured three times to record its potential shift. As shown in Fig. 6D, the RSD was 8.376 % at 0.1 µg/mL of LDL, which showed an acceptable reproducibility for LDL detection.

To study the stability of LDL C-LAPS, The prepared LDLApt/OPD@NGQDs/rGO/ITO electrode was stored in a refrigerator at 4℃, removed at different time, and LDL (0.1 µg/mL) was detected under the same conditions. The experimental results were shown in Fig. 6E. As shown in Fig. 6E, the ΔV was 96.43 % of its initial ΔV on day 3, and the value on day 13 was 74.84 % of its initial ΔV. The results showed that LDL C-LAPS has good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of LDL in human serum sample using the LDL C-LAPS
ce:section.ce:para: In order to investigate the actual effect of LDL C-LAPS, real human serum samples were tested. The serum samples were provided from Guangxi Key Laboratory of Metabolic Disease Research, 924th Hospital of the People’s Liberation Army Joint Logistics Support Force (Guilin, China). Six human serum samples were diluted to 0.1275 μg/mL, 0.1082 μg/mL, 0.1237 μg/mL, 0.1542 μg/mL, 0.1469 μg/mL and 0.1550 μg/mL at a ratio of 1:10000. Three samples were diluted to 0.0817 μg/mL, 0.0806 μg/mL, 0.0815 μg/mL at a ratio of 1:20000. Under optimal conditions, LDL levels in real serum samples were measured by I-V method based on the developed C-LAPS. According to the calibration curve, the LDL concentrations in different samples was deduced and the results are shown in Table 2 . From Table 2, the relative error between the measured value of C-LAPS and the clinic value was within 7.9 %, and the RSD was less than 10 %, which indicates that LDL C-LAPS can be applied to practical testing and has clinical research value.

In summary, we have developed a novel carbon-based aptamer LAPS using OPD@NGQDs/rGO as the photosensitive layer and Indium Tin Oxide (ITO) as the conductive substrate was designed for LDL detection. By combining the advantages of OPD@NGQDs and rGO, an OPD@NGQDs/rGO photosensitive layer with large specific surface area, excellent photoelectric conversion capability, good biocompatibility and mechanical properties was obtained, which contributes to the good performance of the LAPS. The ΔV of LDL C-LAPS showed good linearity with the concentration of LDL in the range of 0.01 μg/mL – 0.15 μg/mL. The sensitivity was 1819.42 mV/μg·mL−1, and the detection limit was 6.43 ng/mL when the signal-to-noise ratio was 3. Moreover, the constructed LDL C-LAPS has good selectivity, stability and reproducibility. The proposed C-LAPS has been applied to detect LDL in serum samples with satisfactory relative error. We believe that this method may be an effective strategy for determination of LDL with potential clinical applications and can improve the sensitivity of ASCVD detection for real-time.
Declaration of Interest Statement.

Herein, a novel carbon-based Light Addressable Potentiometric Sensor (C-LAPS) for determination LDL has been constructed based on o-phenylenediamine modified nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/reduce graphene oxide (OPD@NGQDs/rGO) photosensitive layer. The OPD@NGQDs/rGO photosensitive layer has excellent photoelectric conversion efficiency, large specific surface area and good biocompatibility to enhance the response signal. For the determination LDL by the C-LAPS system, LDL binding to LDL aptamer causes a change in the surface potential of the photosensitive layer, producing a response signal that can be detected by the I-V curve. Under optimal conditions, the C-LAPS had a good linear relationship with LDL concentration in the range of 0.01 μg/mL- 0.15 μg/mL. The sensitivity was 1819 mV/µg·mL−1 and the limit of detection (LOD) was 6.43 ng/mL (S/N = 3). In addition, the C-LAPS showed good specificity, reproducibility, stability and satisfactory relative error for detecting LDL in human serum samples. Therefore, the C-LAPS has good performance and demonstrates potential clinical value.
